initial_claims.description: The contract is named 'Rubixi' but the constructor function is named 'DynamicPyramid'. In Solidity <0.5, constructors must share the contract name; otherwise the function becomes a regular public function. Any attacker can call DynamicPyramid() to set creator = msg.sender, gaining full ownership. The attacker then controls all owner-gated functions: collectAllFees(), collectFeesInEther(), collectPercentOfFees(), changeOwner(), changeMultiplier(), changeFeePercentage().
initial_claims.evidence: Contract name is 'Rubixi' (line 3) but constructor is 'function DynamicPyramid()' (line 12). This mismatch means DynamicPyramid() is a publicly callable function. Exploit: attacker calls DynamicPyramid() -> creator = attacker -> attacker calls collectAllFees() -> drains all collected fees.

verdict.reasoning: The contract is named 'Rubixi' (line 3) but the constructor function is named 'DynamicPyramid' (line 12). In Solidity <0.5, a constructor must share the exact name of the contract. Since 'DynamicPyramid' != 'Rubixi', this function is treated as a regular public function rather than a constructor. This means: (1) The creator variable is never set during deployment (it defaults to address(0)), and (2) Any external attacker can call DynamicPyramid() at any time to set creator = msg.sender. Once the attacker controls creator, they can call collectAllFees() to drain all collected fees, collectFeesInEther() and collectPercentOfFees() for partial fee extraction, changeOwner() to permanently transfer ownership, changeMultiplier() and changeFeePercentage() to manipulate pyramid parameters. The onlyowner modifier checks 'if (msg.sender == creator) _;' — since creator starts as address(0), initially no one has owner access, but the first caller of DynamicPyramid() gains full control. The exploit path is trivially achievable: call DynamicPyramid() then call collectAllFees(). This is the famous Rubixi vulnerability — a real historical exploit. The defender correctly acknowledges no mitigation exists. The harm is to all participants who deposited ETH into the pyramid, as their fees can be drained by the attacker.
verdict.recommendation: Rename the constructor to match the contract name: 'function Rubixi()' instead of 'function DynamicPyramid()'. In Solidity 0.4.22+, use the 'constructor()' keyword to avoid this class of bug entirely. Additionally, add a check in the constructor to prevent re-initialization if upgrading to a newer pattern.
